ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for pork cutlet cheese sauce curry rice; closest defensible anchor is curry rice food product (FOODON_00002809). Pork cutlet and cheese sauce components are not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers rice-based dishes as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Japanese-style pork katsu cheese curry rice in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Japanese curry rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pork cutlet cheese sauce curry rice served in a Singapore Japanese restaurant typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising steamed rice, a deep-fried pork cutlet, Japanese curry sauce, and cheese sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice (cr_id G00010936). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (pork cutlet + cheese sauce + curry rice) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice and curry rice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork cutlet cheese sauce curry rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese curry rice (white rice base, GI ~65–68) and the moderating effect of dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), protein (6.5 g/100g), and fat (4.21 g/100g) from the pork cutlet and cheese sauce components. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: net carbohydrate per serving = (25.19 g/100g × 400 g) − (3.7 g/100g × 400 g) = 100.76 g − 14.8 g = 85.96 g net carb; conservative mixed-meal GL = 65 × 64.0 g / 100 ≈ 41.6, reflecting mixed-meal GI dampening from protein and fat. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g means a 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium (about 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); patients with hypertension should request reduced cheese sauce and curry sauce on the side and limit portion size to 200–250 g to keep sodium intake within DASH-diet targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65) and 25.2 g/100g carbohydrate; partially mitigated by 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100g protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium estimated GI (~65) and moderate carbohydrate content require careful portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes; limiting the serving to 200–250 g, requesting baked or grilled pork instead of deep-fried, and reducing the cheese sauce will lower the glycemic load and the saturated fat intake that may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried pork cutlet and cheese sauce contribute saturated fat and dietary AGEs; sodium 231 mg/100g adds cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried pork cutlet and cheese sauce contribute saturated fat (estimated ~1.5 g/100g) and pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are unfavourable for cardiovascular disease; patients should request grilled or baked pork, reduced cheese sauce, and limit the serving to 200 g to keep sodium and saturated fat within cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (red meat, arachidonic acid) and deep-frying (AGEs) are hard RA disqualifiers; cheese sauce adds saturated fat; potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is not recommended for people with rheumatoid arthritis due to the combination of pork (high arachidonic acid), deep-frying (generates pro-inflammatory AGEs), and cheese sauce (saturated fat); substituting with grilled chicken or fish, removing the cheese sauce, and replacing potato with non-nightshade vegetables would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice is a Japanese-inspired composite dish popular in Singapore's Japanese restaurant and food court scene. It consists of steamed white rice topped with a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet (tonkatsu), a rich Japanese-style curry sauce made with potato, carrot, and onion, and a creamy cheese sauce drizzled over the cutlet. The dish delivers a layered flavour profile: savoury and mildly spiced curry, crispy and juicy pork, and a rich, slightly tangy cheese sauce. The texture contrasts the soft rice and tender curry vegetables with the crunchy breadcrumb coating of the pork. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable 3.7 g dietary fibre from the curry vegetables and 327 mg potassium. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension. The dish has a medium estimated glycemic index (~65) moderated by its fibre and protein content. Clinically, the deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are unfavourable for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease; the cheese sauce adds saturated fat relevant to insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk. Portion control and preparation modifications (grilled pork, reduced sauce) are recommended for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for halal, vegetarian, or vegan diets. Known in Chinese as 猪排芝士酱咖喱饭, in Malay as Nasi Kari Sos Keju Kotlet Babi, and in Japanese as ポークカツチーズソースカレーライス.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010936) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese curry rice, with the estimation basis fully documented. All mandatory multilingual fields for a Japanese-cuisine dish served in Singapore are present, including Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100g) is an analogous estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite data for deep-fried breaded pork cutlet, cheese sauce, and Japanese curry rice components, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this dish. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement if one becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, though it is nutritionally unexpected for a dish containing pork and cheese — this value should be reviewed against the original HPB analytical data. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for Japanese curry rice (white rice base); the mixed-meal composition (fibre 3.7 g, protein 6.5 g, fat 4.21 g per 100g) moderates the GI relative to plain white rice. 5. The dish is not halal due to pork content. 6. The trailing period in the original name 'Pork Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice.' has been removed in the canonical food_name for cleanliness; the original form is preserved in aliases. 7. This dish is Japanese cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual local names and aliases have been added for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi in addition to Japanese to satisfy both the Japanese-cuisine and Singapore-served multilingual requirements.
